front: Composer: composed the first Russian-language opera in the standard repertoire (A Life for the Tsar) and Ruslan and Lyudmila; described by Tchaikovsky as the origin of Russian music
Composer: apocryphally acquainted himself with dissonance by listening to the out-of-tune bells of his native Smolensk
Composer: his song 'The Lark' (from Farewell to St. Petersburg) was arranged for solo piano by Mily Balakirev
Composer: inspired the Mighty Five (Balakirev, Mussorgsky, Borodin, Rimsky-Korsakov, Cui), who sought to create a distinctly Russian musical style
Composer: his 'Patriotic Song' served as Russia's temporary national anthem from 1990 to 2000; founded a namesake prize in 1884 (Tchaikovsky's Romeo and Juliet was an early winner)
Composer: wrote two Spanish overtures — 'Capriccio Brilliante on the Jota Aragonesa' and 'A Night in Madrid' — after travels in Spain
Opera: depicts Ivan Susanin leading a Polish army into the Smolensk forests to their deaths to save Tsar Mikhail Romanov; retitled 'Hammer and Sickle' in the Soviet era
work: A Life for the Tsar (Ivan Susanin)

front: Opera: based on a Pushkin poem; the knight Ruslan rescues his abducted bride from a giant talking head; overture uses piano and harp to imitate the gusli; Act II contains Farlaf's Rondo

Tone Poem: orchestral fantasy based on the bridal song 'Izza gor' and the folk dance Kamarinskaya; the first orchestral work based entirely on Russian folk songs; Tchaikovsky called it 'the acorn from which the oak of Russian music grew'
